statement: Icelandic public opinion on EU membership has swung dramatically since the 2009 application. Support peaked at approximately 55% in early 2009, during the financial crisis when EU/euro membership was seen as a stability anchor. By 2013, support had fallen to approximately 25% as the economy recovered and the centre-right government suspended negotiations. Support gradually recovered to ~30% by 2018, ~35% by 2022, and ~45% by late 2025. The 2024 Althingi vote to hold a binding referendum re-energised the debate. Throughout this period, the proportion of undecided voters ranged from 15–25%, representing the decisive swing group.
source_name: Gallup Ísland — EU membership polling series 2009–2026; Capacent/MMR — supplementary polls
caveats: Long-term polling comparisons must account for changes in question wording, polling methodology (phone vs online), and polling firms. The crisis-era spike may reflect 'flight to safety' sentiment rather than deep commitment to EU integration.

statement: Norway held its first EU membership referendum on 25 September 1972, with 53.5% voting against and 46.5% in favour, on a turnout of 79.2%. The campaign was dominated by concerns about fisheries, agriculture, and national sovereignty — issues strikingly similar to Iceland's current debate. The 'No' camp was a broad coalition of farmers, fishers, left-wing parties, and peripheral/rural communities, while the 'Yes' camp drew support from business, urban professionals, and the Labour Party leadership. The result led Norway to negotiate a free trade agreement with the EEC instead, prefiguring the EEA model adopted in 1994.
source_name: Statistics Norway — Election and referendum statistics; Bjørklund (2005) — 'Mot strømmen'
caveats: The 1972 referendum occurred before the EEA framework existed, so the counterfactual was different from Iceland's current situation. Norway's petroleum discoveries in the late 1960s bolstered the case for independence from the EEC. The social and political cleavages in 1972 Norway may not map directly onto 2026 Iceland.

statement: Norway held its second EU membership referendum on 28 November 1994, with 52.2% voting against and 47.8% in favour, on a turnout of 89.0% — the highest turnout for any Norwegian national vote. The margin narrowed compared to 1972, with urban areas and business interests strongly pro-EU while rural and northern Norway remained firmly opposed. The fisheries issue was again decisive: coastal communities feared the Common Fisheries Policy would destroy their livelihoods. Norway subsequently joined the EEA (effective 1 January 1994, before the referendum) as its alternative to full membership — the same framework Iceland currently participates in.
source_name: Statistics Norway — Referendum results 1994; Archer (2005) — 'Norway outside the European Union'
caveats: Norway's oil wealth gave it a stronger economic basis for rejecting EU membership than Iceland possesses. The narrowing margin between 1972 and 1994 suggested a trend toward 'Yes', but subsequent polling has shown no sustained majority for EU membership in Norway. Norway's EEA experience since 1994 is the closest parallel to Iceland's situation.

statement: Support for EU membership in Iceland shows a clear generational gradient. Among 18–29-year-olds, approximately 58% support EU membership (2025 data), compared to 48% among 30–44-year-olds, 38% among 45–59-year-olds, and 28% among those 60+. This pattern is consistent across multiple polls and polling firms. The generational divide is attributed to younger Icelanders having grown up with free movement, Erasmus exchanges, and globalised media, while older generations have stronger emotional ties to independence-era sovereignty concerns and memories of the Cod Wars with Britain. Critically, turnout among younger voters is historically 15–20 percentage points lower than among older voters in Icelandic elections.
source_name: Félagsvísindastofnun Háskóla Íslands — EU attitudes by age cohort (2025); Gallup Ísland
caveats: Age-based subsamples have larger margins of error. The generational divide may narrow as the referendum campaign intensifies — older voters may be more persuadable by economic arguments, while younger voters' support may be shallower. Turnout differentials could be decisive: if youth turnout matches older cohorts, the 'Yes' side gains significantly.

statement: EU membership support varies significantly by region in Iceland. The capital area (Höfuðborgarsvæðið, ~65% of population) shows approximately 50–52% support, while rural and coastal regions show 30–35% support. The strongest opposition is in Vestfirðir (West Fjords, ~70% opposed) and Austurland (East Iceland, ~60% opposed), both regions economically dependent on fisheries. Akureyri and the northeast show intermediate levels (~40% support). This urban-rural divide mirrors the Norwegian pattern from both 1972 and 1994 referendums. The concentration of 'Yes' voters in the capital means that national polling figures can overstate support in the sense that 'Yes' votes are geographically inefficient for generating broad-based legitimacy.
source_name: Gallup Ísland — Regional breakdown polls; Capacent — Regional economic surveys
caveats: Regional polling subsamples are small (50–200 respondents per region), resulting in wide confidence intervals. Migration patterns between regions and the capital area may shift the composition of regional electorates. Some regions have too few respondents for reliable estimates.

statement: Support for EU membership in Iceland is strongly correlated with economic distress. During the 2008–2010 financial crisis, when GDP contracted by 10.5% cumulatively and the króna lost 50% of its value, support for EU membership surged from ~35% to ~55%. As the economy recovered (2011–2016), support fell back to 25–30%. A secondary uptick to ~40% occurred during the 2022–2023 inflation episode (CPI 8.3–8.7%). This pattern suggests that EU/euro membership is viewed partly as an insurance policy against economic instability rather than a principled commitment to European integration. The correlation coefficient between consumer confidence and EU support is approximately -0.6 (negative: lower confidence = higher EU support).
source_name: Gallup Ísland — EU polls cross-referenced with Hagstofa consumer confidence index
caveats: Correlation is not causation — other factors (government composition, media coverage, international events) also influence EU support. The current referendum occurs during a period of moderate economic performance, which may reduce crisis-driven support. If the economy weakens before August 2026, the 'Yes' side could benefit.

statement: The wording of EU-related poll questions significantly affects measured support. When asked 'Should Iceland apply for EU membership?', support is typically 3–5 percentage points higher than when asked 'Should Iceland join the EU?', and 5–8 points higher than when asked 'Are you in favour of EU membership?'. The application question implies a process with future decision points, while the membership question implies finality. The binding referendum question — 'Viljið þér að Ísland gerist aðili að Evrópusambandinu?' ('Do you want Iceland to become a member of the European Union?') — uses the direct membership framing, which historically produces lower support levels. Pollsters using different wordings have produced divergent results on the same day by up to 10 percentage points.
source_name: Félagsvísindastofnun — Methodology notes on EU polling; comparison of Gallup, MMR, and Maskína polls
caveats: Question wording effects are well-documented in referendum polling internationally. The official referendum question was set by legislation and cannot be changed. Pre-referendum polls using different wording should be interpreted with caution when predicting the actual vote.

statement: EU support varies dramatically by party affiliation. Among Viðreisn voters, ~90% support EU membership; among Samfylkingin voters, ~75%. Píratar voters show ~55% support, and Left-Green voters ~45%. On the opposing side, Sjálfstæðisflokkur voters show ~25% support, Framsóknarflokkur voters ~20%, Miðflokkur voters ~10%, and Flokkur fólksins voters ~10%. These figures (from late 2025 polling) suggest that the referendum will cut across party lines, with significant minorities within each party diverging from the official party position. The ~25% of Independence Party voters who support membership is particularly significant given that party's large vote share (~22% in recent elections).
source_name: Gallup Ísland — EU support by party affiliation (November 2025)
caveats: Party-based subsamples have larger margins of error. Voters may shift party allegiance before the referendum, particularly if parties take stronger stances. The gap between party leadership positions and voter preferences suggests the referendum outcome will not simply reflect the parliamentary balance of power.

statement: Pre-accession polling in recent EU member states shows a pattern of rising support as accession approaches. In Croatia (accession 2013), support rose from ~45% in 2010 to ~67% at the January 2012 referendum. In Austria (1994), support was ~55% in early polling and reached 66.6% at the referendum. In Finland (1994), support was ~40% in 1992 and reached 56.9% at the October 1994 referendum. In Sweden (1994), support hovered around 45% until a late surge produced a narrow 52.3% Yes. The pattern suggests that undecided voters tend to break towards 'Yes' as the vote approaches, but this effect is not universal — Norway's 1994 result confounded predictions of a late Yes swing.
source_name: Eurobarometer — Pre-accession surveys; national referendum results
caveats: Each accession referendum occurred in unique political and economic circumstances. The 'late swing to Yes' pattern may reflect status quo bias (voting for the government's preferred option) rather than genuine conversion. Iceland's binding referendum is structurally different from most accession referendums, which occurred after negotiations were complete.

statement: Undecided voters represent approximately 15–20% of the Icelandic electorate on the EU question as of early 2026. Analysis of undecided voter demographics shows they are disproportionately female (55–60%), aged 30–50, with moderate education levels, and concentrated in the capital region's suburbs and satellite towns (Kópavogur, Hafnarfjörður, Garðabær). Their top concerns are economic stability (particularly housing costs and inflation), followed by fisheries and sovereignty. Undecided voters tend to have lower political engagement and are less likely to turn out. Historical comparison with the Norwegian and Austrian referendums suggests undecided voters break approximately 55–60% towards 'Yes' in the final weeks, but this depends heavily on campaign dynamics and late-breaking events.
source_name: Félagsvísindastofnun Háskóla Íslands — Undecided voter survey (2025); Maskína — swing voter analysis
caveats: Undecided voter analysis is inherently uncertain — respondents who say they are undecided may lean one way but be reluctant to state it. The assumption that undecided voters break towards 'Yes' is based on international precedent but not guaranteed for Iceland. Turnout among undecided voters may be lower than among committed voters, reducing their actual impact.
